itemDefinition.title: OTA Update Manager ECU
itemDefinition.boundary: ECU responsible for receiving, validating, and applying over-the-air software updates across in-vehicle networks
itemDefinition.architecture: ARM Cortex-A, AUTOSAR Adaptive, Ethernet/IP backbone, cellular modem for cloud connectivity
assumptions: HSM present and provisioned at manufacturing
Secure boot enabled on all receiving ECUs
Backend OTA server operates within OEM security perimeter

damageScenarios.title: Unauthorized software deployed via tampered OTA package
damageScenarios.description: Malicious actor delivers a tampered or malicious software update, causing degraded functionality, safety hazards, or persistent compromise of vehicle ECUs

damageScenarios.title: Downgrade to vulnerable software version
damageScenarios.description: Attacker forces rollback to an older software version with known vulnerabilities, re-exposing previously remediated security issues

damageScenarios.title: OTA signing key compromise enabling persistent unauthorized updates
damageScenarios.description: Attacker obtains OTA signing key material and can sign arbitrary software packages, leading to persistent and undetectable vehicle compromise
affectedRoadUsers: all users of affected vehicle fleet

damageScenarios.title: Man-in-the-middle of OTA channel leads to corrupted update
damageScenarios.description: Network attacker intercepts and modifies the OTA communication channel, injecting a malicious update payload

damageScenarios.title: OTA channel disruption prevents critical safety patch delivery
damageScenarios.description: Denial of service on the OTA channel prevents delivery of time-critical security patches, leaving vehicles exposed to known vulnerabilities

threatScenarios.title: Injection of unsigned OTA package via compromised backend
threatScenarios.cause: Attacker compromises backend OTA server or intercepts update distribution, replacing legitimate package with tampered version lacking valid signature

threatScenarios.title: Forced rollback attack via crafted update metadata
threatScenarios.cause: Attacker crafts update metadata indicating an older version is newer, causing the ECU to downgrade to a vulnerable software version

threatScenarios.title: Exfiltration of HSM-protected signing keys
threatScenarios.cause: Side-channel attack or physical extraction of HSM yields signing key material usable for crafting valid malicious update packages

threatScenarios.title: TLS downgrade or MITM on OTA channel
threatScenarios.cause: Attacker performs protocol downgrade attack on TLS session or exploits weak cipher negotiation to intercept and modify OTA traffic

attackPaths.title: Backend compromise leading to malicious OTA package delivery
steps: Gain access to OEM backend OTA infrastructure via credential theft or RCE
Replace or inject a malicious update package in the distribution pipeline
ECU receives package, signature check fails or is bypassed
Malicious software executes on vehicle ECU

attackPaths.title: Version rollback via metadata manipulation
steps: Intercept or modify OTA metadata in transit
Set version field to trigger rollback logic
ECU installs older version without anti-rollback enforcement

attackFeasibilityRatings.rationale: Requires sophisticated backend compromise and exploitation of update pipeline
Rollback attacks are well-understood; feasibility depends on anti-rollback enforcement

impactRatings.rationale: Unauthorized code execution on safety-critical ECUs can lead to loss of vehicle control and broad fleet impact
Re-exposure of patched vulnerabilities may enable further attacks with safety implications

riskValues.rationale: High impact combined with high but not insurmountable attack feasibility yields HIGH overall risk
Medium feasibility with high impact results in HIGH risk; must be mitigated

controls.title: Mandatory cryptographic signature verification for all OTA packages
Mutual TLS with certificate pinning for OTA channel
HSM-based key storage and signing operations
OTA update audit log with tamper-evident storage

riskTreatmentDecisions.justification: Cryptographic controls reduce the feasibility of injecting unsigned packages; backend protections address infrastructure attack vectors
Hardware anti-rollback counters prevent version downgrade regardless of metadata manipulation

cybersecurityGoals.title: Ensure authenticity and integrity of all OTA software packages
cybersecurityGoals.description: The OTA Update Manager SHALL only accept and apply software packages that have been cryptographically signed by the OEM's authorized code-signing authority. Any package failing verification MUST be rejected and the update aborted.
cybersecurityGoals.acceptanceCriteria: 100% of OTA packages are verified against OEM public key certificate chain before installation; unsigned or invalid packages are rejected and logged with no partial installation

cybersecurityGoals.title: Prevent unauthorized software version rollback
cybersecurityGoals.description: The OTA Update Manager and target ECU bootloaders SHALL enforce monotonic version counters such that previously superseded software versions cannot be reinstalled, preventing re-introduction of patched vulnerabilities.
cybersecurityGoals.acceptanceCriteria: Anti-rollback counter is incremented with each security-relevant update; attempts to install versions with lower counter values are rejected by bootloader

cybersecurityGoals.title: Protect OTA signing key material from unauthorized access
cybersecurityGoals.description: Private key material used for OTA package signing SHALL be stored exclusively in hardware security modules (HSM) with no plaintext export capability. All signing operations SHALL occur within the HSM boundary.
cybersecurityGoals.acceptanceCriteria: OTA signing private keys are generated and stored in FIPS 140-2 Level 3 or equivalent HSM; no key export interface is enabled; key usage is logged and auditable

cybersecurityGoals.title: Ensure confidentiality and integrity of OTA communication channel
cybersecurityGoals.description: All communications between the vehicle OTA client and the OEM backend server SHALL be protected by mutually authenticated TLS with certificate pinning to prevent interception or modification in transit.
cybersecurityGoals.acceptanceCriteria: OTA channel uses TLS 1.3 minimum with mutual authentication; certificate pinning prevents connection to unauthorized servers; all TLS handshake failures are logged

cybersecurityClaims.title: OTA signature verification is correctly implemented
cybersecurityClaims.claim: The OTA Update Manager correctly verifies the ECDSA P-256 signature of every received update package against the OEM's provisioned root CA certificate chain before initiating any installation, and rejects any package that fails this check.
cybersecurityClaims.rationale: Verified by penetration testing (OTA-PENTEST-2026-Q1) and static code analysis (SAST-OTA-2026-003); all test cases in OTA-TC-SIG-001 through OTA-TC-SIG-025 pass

cybersecurityClaims.title: Anti-rollback enforcement prevents downgrade attacks
cybersecurityClaims.claim: The OTA bootloader anti-rollback counter implementation correctly prevents installation of any software package with a version counter value less than or equal to the currently stored counter value.
cybersecurityClaims.rationale: Verified by functional testing of rollback scenarios; hardware counter implemented in OTP memory is non-decreasing by construction

cybersecurityClaims.title: OTA channel TLS configuration meets minimum security requirements
cybersecurityClaims.claim: The OTA communication channel enforces TLS 1.3 as minimum protocol version, uses only approved cipher suites, and implements certificate pinning against the OEM's backend certificate authority.
cybersecurityClaims.rationale: TLS configuration audited by external security team; cipher suite configuration reviewed and approved

cybersecurityRequirements.title: OTA package signature verification SHALL use OEM root CA
cybersecurityRequirements.description: The OTA Update Manager SHALL verify the digital signature of each received update package using the ECDSA P-256 algorithm against the OEM root CA certificate pre-provisioned in the ECU's secure storage. Verification SHALL occur before any portion of the package is written to flash storage.
cybersecurityRequirements.allocatedTo: OTA Update Manager ECU — update reception module

cybersecurityRequirements.title: Failed signature verification SHALL abort update and generate security event
cybersecurityRequirements.description: If OTA package signature verification fails for any reason, the update process SHALL be immediately aborted, no package data SHALL be written to non-volatile storage, and a security event SHALL be logged to the vehicle security event log with the package hash, timestamp, and failure reason.
cybersecurityRequirements.allocatedTo: OTA Update Manager ECU — update reception module

cybersecurityRequirements.title: Bootloader anti-rollback counter SHALL be enforced in hardware
cybersecurityRequirements.description: Each target ECU bootloader SHALL maintain a monotonically increasing anti-rollback counter stored in OTP (One-Time Programmable) memory or equivalent hardware. Software updates SHALL only be accepted if the update package version counter is strictly greater than the stored counter value.

cybersecurityRequirements.title: OTA signing private keys SHALL reside exclusively in HSM
cybersecurityRequirements.description: The private key material used to sign OTA update packages SHALL be generated within and permanently stored in a Hardware Security Module certified to FIPS 140-2 Level 3 or higher. The HSM SHALL NOT provide any interface for exporting private key material in plaintext form.

cybersecurityRequirements.title: OTA channel SHALL enforce TLS 1.3 with certificate pinning
cybersecurityRequirements.description: The OTA communication channel between the vehicle and the OEM backend SHALL use TLS 1.3 as the minimum protocol version. The OTA client SHALL implement certificate pinning against the OEM backend's certificate authority public key to prevent connection to unauthorized servers.
cybersecurityRequirements.allocatedTo: OTA Update Manager ECU — communication module

cybersecurityRequirements.title: OTA audit log SHALL record all update attempts
cybersecurityRequirements.description: The OTA Update Manager SHALL maintain a tamper-evident audit log recording all update attempts including: package identifier, version, timestamp, verification outcome, and whether installation was completed or aborted. Log entries SHALL be cryptographically chained to detect tampering.
cybersecurityRequirements.allocatedTo: OTA Update Manager ECU — audit module

cybersecurityConcept.title: Defense-in-depth OTA security architecture
cybersecurityConcept.description: The OTA security architecture employs multiple independent layers of protection ensuring that compromise of any single layer is insufficient to deploy unauthorized software. The layers are: (1) authenticated and encrypted transport channel, (2) cryptographic package signature verification, (3) hardware-enforced anti-rollback, and (4) audit logging. This aligns with ISO/SAE 21434 Clause 9.4 cybersecurity concept requirements.
designDecisions: ECDSA P-256 selected for package signing due to performance characteristics on embedded targets and strong security margin
TLS 1.3 mandated to eliminate known vulnerabilities in earlier protocol versions (POODLE, BEAST, etc.)
Hardware OTP anti-rollback counter chosen over software counter to prevent bypass via flash manipulation
HSM-based signing at backend eliminates software key exposure risks during signing operations
Certificate pinning implemented at OTA client to reduce exposure to PKI infrastructure compromise
Audit log uses hash-chaining to provide tamper evidence without requiring specialized hardware
assumptions: OEM PKI infrastructure maintains adequate security controls for root CA private keys
HSM at backend is operated and maintained according to OEM key management policy
Target ECUs have sufficient flash memory to maintain previous version for rollback recovery
Cellular network availability may be intermittent; OTA client handles partial download resumption securely

cybersecurityConcept.title: Organizational OTA key management and governance process
cybersecurityConcept.description: An organizational process governs the entire lifecycle of OTA code-signing keys including generation, rotation, revocation, and destruction. This process ensures continuity of update capability while minimizing key exposure duration and providing rapid response to key compromise events.
designDecisions: Dual-control ceremony required for all root CA key operations with minimum two authorized personnel
Subordinate signing keys rotated annually with 90-day overlap period for in-flight updates
Key compromise response procedure defined with maximum 4-hour revocation and re-issuance SLA
Hardware tokens required for all personnel with signing authority
assumptions: OEM security team maintains 24/7 on-call capability for key compromise response
HSM vendor provides maintenance and replacement within SLA during key ceremony windows

cybersecurityConcept.title: Fail-secure update recovery and rollback strategy
cybersecurityConcept.description: In the event of an interrupted or failed update installation, the system SHALL revert to the last known-good software state. This recovery concept ensures vehicle operability is maintained while preventing installation of partially written or corrupted update images.
designDecisions: A/B partition scheme used in target ECU flash layout to enable atomic update swap
Bootloader commits to new partition only after successful signature verification and integrity check
Anti-rollback counter is incremented only after successful boot from new partition is confirmed
Failed updates trigger automatic revert to previous partition without requiring OTA retry
assumptions: Target ECU flash provides sufficient storage for A/B partition scheme
Power loss during flash write handled by hardware ECC and write-verify mechanisms

relationships.justification: Cybersecurity goal CSG-OTA-01 directly addresses the threat scenario of injecting unsigned OTA packages
Cybersecurity goal CSG-OTA-02 directly addresses the rollback attack threat scenario
Cybersecurity goal CSG-OTA-01 was derived from the HIGH risk value assessment of the unauthorized package injection scenario
Cybersecurity goal CSG-OTA-02 was derived from the HIGH risk value assessment of the rollback attack scenario
Requirement CSR-OTA-01 was derived from cybersecurity goal CSG-OTA-01 to specify the concrete technical mechanism
Requirement CSR-OTA-03 was derived from cybersecurity goal CSG-OTA-02 for hardware anti-rollback enforcement
Control CTRL-OTA-SIG implements the mandatory signature verification required by CSR-OTA-01
Control CTRL-OTA-ARV implements hardware anti-rollback enforcement required by CSR-OTA-03
Claim CSC-OTA-01 provides verified evidence that cybersecurity goal CSG-OTA-01 has been achieved
Cybersecurity concept CC-OTA-01 describes the architectural approach by which CSG-OTA-01 is achieved
Mandatory signature verification directly mitigates the unsigned package injection threat
Hardware anti-rollback counter directly mitigates the version downgrade threat scenario
